metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that DET enrichment (churn_90d, reversibility surface flag) must be patched into the *verification* dict at `verifs[i]['severity_attributes']` (the dict `pass3_decide` reads as `attrs`), not into the finding dict `findings[i]`, and add an AC or test asserting that DET fields set in the verification attrs are read by `impact_code`.
findings.finding: The DET enrichment merge target is ambiguous: the plan says 'merges them into that finding's severity_attributes BEFORE pass3_over_findings', but pass3_decide reads attrs exclusively from the *verification* dict (verification.get('severity_attributes', {})), not from the finding dict. In pass3_over_findings, the finding dict f is only accessed for threshold_for(f.get('criteria', [])); the attrs dict that impact_code receives comes from verifs.get(i). If the implementer writes DET data (churn_90d-derived freq_mult, reversibility surface flag) into findings[i]['severity_attributes'] (the finding's own dict), impact_code will silently receive an attrs dict with no DET signals — the freq_mult and reversibility_floor would then compute from defaults rather than the actual git/path data.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'DET ENRICHMENT: code_review_decide computes churn_90d (git) + the reversibility surface (changed-file paths) per finding and merges them into that finding's severity_attributes BEFORE pass3_over_findings, so impact_code(attrs) reads both the LLM binaries and the DET signals from one dict.'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's DET ENRICHMENT description to: 'code_review_decide computes churn_90d and the reversibility surface per finding and patches those scalar signals into each finding's corresponding entry in the verifications map (verifs[i]["severity_attributes"]) before calling pass3_over_findings — the same dict impact_code(attrs) receives.' Add a unit-test assertion: given a verification with churn_90d=30 in severity_attributes, impact_code produces a higher maint_lane than one with churn_90d=0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a shell proving command for the ADR deliverable in AC3, e.g. `test -f docs/adr/*two-lane-impact*.md && wc -l docs/adr/*two-lane-impact*.md` (or a CI file-existence lint), so the ADR's presence is verifiable without manual inspection.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py -v`) verifies dispatch isolation and plan-review unchanged, but does NOT verify that the ADR file under `docs/adr/` was actually written — the ADR deliverable has no proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'an ADR under docs/adr/ records the model. Proving command: .venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py -v'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3's proving commands: `find docs/adr -name '*two-lane*' | grep -q . && echo ADR_EXISTS` or a lightweight CI linter that fails if the named ADR file is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete `code_review_verification_model` builder function to the plan (mirroring `plan_review_verification_model`'s Pydantic pattern) with explicit field types, `description=` strings, and defaults for all 10 tier-tagged binaries (bool, default=False), `trigger_likelihood` (str, description='common|sometimes|rare', default='common'), `silent_failure` (bool, default=False), and `escapes_automation` (bool, default=False).
findings.finding: `code_review_verification_model` and `CodeSeverityAttrs` are referenced as the new schema to be registered in `code_review/contracts.py`, but no Pydantic model builder body is specified in the plan — the existing `code_review/contracts.py` has no such registration and the plan provides only a prose field list, leaving the concrete field types, description strings, and defaults unspecified for an executor.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: '(i) register a `code_review_verification` contract in `code_review/contracts.py` -> code_review_verification_model'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan an explicit description of the prompt-content changes required in `code-review-verify.md`: per-field instructions for each of the 10 tier-tagged binaries (with their carve-out definitions), `trigger_likelihood` (with the common|sometimes|rare vocabulary and guidance on when to use each), `silent_failure`, and `escapes_automation`.
findings.finding: The verify prompt `code-review-verify.md` is not updated in the plan to instruct the LLM on the 10 new tier-tagged binary fields, `trigger_likelihood`, `silent_failure`, or `escapes_automation` — only the `outputs:` front-matter key change is named. The LLM will receive a schema with new fields but no in-prompt guidance, causing it to emit defaults (false/common) for every new field on every finding.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: '(iii) update the `outputs:` front-matter key in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-verify.md` from `verification` to `code_review_verification`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the per-finding file-attribution rule for DET enrichment: how `churn_90d` and the reversibility surface are extracted from a finding's `location` field (including the fallback for empty/ambiguous locations), and whether the enrichment is keyed on the finding's attributed file or on the global changed-file list.
findings.finding: The DET enrichment placement for `churn_90d` and the reversibility surface detection is ambiguous: the plan says `code_review_decide` merges these into `severity_attributes` BEFORE `pass3_over_findings`, but `code_review_decide` currently receives `verifications` as a flat list from the `verify` step's output — the plan does not specify how the per-finding file attribution (which file path maps to which finding index) is established, given that findings may span multiple files and the verifier output does not include file paths.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'DET ENRICHMENT: `code_review_decide` computes `churn_90d` (git) + the reversibility surface (changed-file paths) per finding and merges them into that finding's severity_attributes BEFORE `pass3_over_findings`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of the five serious-tier binaries, specify the charitable carve-out text that belongs in the verify prompt: the exact condition under which the binary should remain false despite surface-level relevance (e.g. for data_loss: 'false if any recovery path — backup, rollback, retry — exists; true only for irrecoverable loss with no recovery mechanism').
findings.finding: The 'charitable carve-outs' for each serious-tier binary (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement) are referenced as being 'in the verify-prompt definition' but are never actually specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: 'What' section: 'Each serious binary carries its charitable carve-out in its verify-prompt definition'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git` flag form for the churn_90d count (e.g., `git rev-list --count --since='90 days ago' -- <path>` as the portable form, or `git log --format='' --since='90 days ago' -- <path> | wc -l`) and note that a non-zero but truncated result from a shallow clone is NOT the same as the stated fallback (git-unavailable ⇒ churn=0) — the shallow-clone case should be documented as a known limitation or detected via `git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `git log` (DET) to compute `churn_90d` inside `code_review_decide` but does not name the exact flag surface — specifically, whether to use `--since='90 days ago'`, `--after`, `--format=''`, `git rev-list --count`, or a pipe-count pattern. The `git log --count` shorthand exists only in Git ≥ 2.28 and is not universal; `git rev-list --count --since='90 days ago' -- <path>` is the portable form. Without pinning the flag surface the implementor must guess, risking a flag-level mismatch (e.g., `--count` silently returning nothing on older git) that would produce incorrect churn values without failing loudly, defeating the fallback's intent.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — DET ENRICHMENT paragraph: 'churn_90d (git) … per finding'
findings.suggested_fix: In the DET ENRICHMENT section, replace 'git log, DET' with the concrete command: `git rev-list --count --since='90 days ago' -- <path>` (portable, no pipe); note that a `CalledProcessError` or `OSError` triggers the churn=0 fallback, but a shallow clone that returns a low count does not and is a known under-count accepted as best-effort.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing the `freq_mult` formula shape, add a brief prior-art note to the ADR (or a 'Rationale' subsection in the plan) citing either (a) internal corpus data showing churn correlates with maint-lane finding severity, or (b) a reference to change-proneness research (e.g., file churn as a defect predictor), and record the chosen window/cap/floor as calibration-adjustable rather than architecturally fixed.
findings.finding: The git-churn-based `freq_mult` sub-model (`0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30`, 90-day window, saturation at 30 commits) is introduced as a novel architectural sub-component with no prior-art citation, no reference to change-proneness research, and no internal empirical basis cited. The formula *shape* (linear saturation, specific window/cap) is committed by this plan while constant-tuning is deferred to a calibration child, meaning the architecture is fixed without establishing that the churn signal is informative at the asserted formula shape.
findings.location: What section — CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER paragraph; also AC-1 (formula in AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR and/or the 'What' section, add a 'Rationale for freq_mult shape' subsection: state that the 90-day window / 30-commit cap are initial calibration-friendly defaults (not empirically validated), reference that change-proneness literature supports churn as a defect predictor directionally, and explicitly make all three constants (window, cap, floor) configurable parameters rather than literals in the formula — so the calibration child can tune the shape, not just the tier values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `churn_90d` computation in `code_review_decide` MUST use `run_git` from `rebar._store.gitutil` (already imported in `code_review/assemble.py`) rather than a new raw `subprocess.run` call, to avoid re-introducing the hand-rolled-wrapper drift `run_git` was built to fix.
findings.finding: The plan's DET enrichment specifies `churn_90d` computed via 'git log, DET' without directing implementors to use the existing shared `run_git` wrapper (`rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`). The codebase's own `gitutil.py` docstring explains it was created specifically to 'consolidate the dozen hand-rolled `_git()` wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each.' The same `code_review/assemble.py` file that `code_review_decide` sits beside already imports `run_git` from this shared helper. Leaving the churn computation unanchored to `run_git` risks an implementor writing a new raw `subprocess.run(["git", ...])` call, re-creating the exact duplication problem `run_git` was introduced to fix.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'DET ENRICHMENT: code_review_decide computes churn_90d (git)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Implementation mechanics / DET ENRICHMENT bullet: 'Use `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (already imported in `code_review/assemble.py`) for the `git log` invocation, consistent with all other git calls in this package.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py` to the Scope as an edit target: export `impact_code` from it (matching the pattern of `impact_plan` at line 49) so `code_review/workflow_ops.py` can import it via `review_kernel.impact_code`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py` is not listed as an edit target in the Scope section, but it must export `impact_code` for `code_review_decide` (in `workflow_ops.py`) to import it via the `review_kernel` package — mirroring how `impact_plan` is exported.
findings.location: Scope section; `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `impact_code` to the import list in `review_kernel/__init__.py` (alongside `impact_plan`) and to `__all__`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Group all calibration-sensitive constants (tier values, trigger_likelihood multipliers, freq_mult formula params, amp values, reversibility_floor) in a single named constants block (e.g., a `CODE_REVIEW_IMPACT_CONSTANTS` dict or a dedicated section at the top of `decide.py`) so the calibration child can change them in one place.
findings.finding: Calibration-sensitive constants (tier values 0.3/0.6/0.9, trigger_likelihood multipliers 1.0/0.6/0.25, freq_mult formula saturation at churn=30, amp values 1.0/0.8, reversibility_floor 0.6) are embedded inline with no plan to group them in a named constants section or config — the calibration child (raptorial-galloping-dragon) will need to iterate on these, but their scattered hardcoding makes that iteration fragile.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics + calibration data / Scope / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `CODE_REVIEW_IMPACT_CONSTANTS` dataclass or module-level dict at the top of `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` (or a sibling `impact_constants.py`) containing all tier values, multiplier values, and formula parameters. Reference these by name in `impact_code`. Document in the ADR that these constants are expected to evolve via the calibration child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit stale-reference audit step (e.g., `grep -r 'output_schema.*verification' src/ tests/ docs/`) to the implementation checklist, or add an AC asserting no remaining references to `output_schema: verification` in the code-review gate path.
findings.finding: The schema rename from `verification` to `code_review_verification` is wired in THREE named places, but the plan does not commit to a stale-reference audit of other potential consumers (e.g., integration tests asserting on the old schema key, CLAUDE.md or README snippets referencing `verification`, other YAML files that import or reference the schema name).
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — wiring paragraph: '(i) register… (ii) change… (iii) update…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-review file-path memoization (e.g., a dict cache keyed on file path) so `churn_90d` is fetched at most once per unique file across all findings in a single review run.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `churn_90d` computation per finding via `git log` without mentioning deduplication by file path. Multiple findings referencing the same file would each trigger a separate `git log` subprocess, producing an N-findings × git-call pattern rather than N-unique-files × git-call.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics: 'code_review_decide computes churn_90d (git) + the reversibility surface (changed-file paths) per finding'
findings.suggested_fix: In `code_review_decide`, collect all unique file paths from findings, run `git log` once per unique path, store results in a dict, then look up churn_90d per finding from that dict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log line (DEBUG/WARNING level) when the git fallback fires (unavailable/shallow clone) and when `trigger_likelihood` or detection booleans are absent/defaulted, so operators can detect systematic degradation.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new DET enrichment step (git subprocess) and new LLM-emitted fields (`trigger_likelihood`, `silent_failure`, `escapes_automation`) but specifies no observability for their failure or absence — the git fallback is silent, and no log/metric/trace is mentioned when the fallback is invoked or when expected fields are absent from the verifier output.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics + Reliability overlay
findings.suggested_fix: In `code_review_decide`, emit `logger.warning('churn_90d fallback: git unavailable for %s, using freq_mult=0.5', file_path)` on the except branch; in `impact_code`, emit a debug log when `trigger_likelihood` is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration or schema-dispatch smoke test (e.g. a fixture-driven test that runs the verify step against a synthetic diff and asserts the output dict contains the expected CodeSeverityAttrs fields) so that a broken YAML/markdown/contracts wiring causes a test failure rather than a silent zero-impact fallback.
findings.finding: The three named schema-wiring cutover changes — (i) registering `code_review_verification` in contracts.py, (ii) switching `output_schema` in code-review.yaml, (iii) updating `outputs:` in code-review-verify.md — have no integration or smoke test verifying the dispatch chain works end-to-end. All AC proving commands point exclusively to `test_impact_code.py`, which tests the pure `impact_code(attrs)` function given a pre-populated dict and will pass even if the YAML/markdown wiring is wrong. If the wiring is broken, all new binary fields default to false, every lane = 0, every impact = 0, and the suite goes green while the defaulted code_review_verification path is silently dead.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — three named wiring places; AC items 1–3 (all proving commands: tests/unit/test_impact_code.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or a named test (e.g. `tests/integration/test_code_review_verify_schema.py`) that loads `code-review.yaml`, resolves `output_schema`, and asserts it equals `code_review_verification`; similarly assert that `code_review_verification_model` in contracts.py declares each new binary field. This can be a structural assertion (load+inspect the schema object) rather than a full LLM call, making it fast and deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the fixture path `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` — either create the `tests/unit/fixtures/` directory as a new convention (add a note explaining the divergence from `tests/fixtures/`) or move the fixture to `tests/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` and update the AC path accordingly.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/fixtures/` directory does not exist in the repo; the plan specifies `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` as a target path, but there is no `tests/unit/fixtures/` subdirectory — the existing fixture convention uses `tests/fixtures/` (one level up).
findings.location: AC4: `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`
findings.suggested_fix: Place the fixture at `tests/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` to match the established pattern; update AC4 and the test's path reference accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one canonical carve-out sentence per serious-tier binary (e.g. for `safety_net_removal_without_replacement`: 'Answer false if an equivalent safety net is introduced in the same diff') directly in the plan or a referenced prompt-template appendix.
findings.finding: The charitable carve-out text for each serious-tier binary is never specified — the plan states 'each serious binary carries its charitable carve-out in its verify-prompt definition' but provides no carve-out wording for any of the six serious binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement, contract_drift/hidden_invariant). An executor must invent all carve-outs, making this a real design decision deferred to implementation.
findings.location: What — CARVED SUB-QUESTION SET bullet; Implementation mechanics — 'Each serious binary carries its charitable carve-out in its verify-prompt definition'
findings.suggested_fix: For each serious binary, add one sentence of the form 'Answer false if [the disqualifying condition]' inline in the CARVED SUB-QUESTION SET table, mirroring the pattern used in the plan-review impact redesign.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC4 how the old-model baseline gap is obtained (e.g. 'each fixture row includes a pre-computed `old_impact` float field, computed once via the existing `impact_plan` path and stored'; or 'the test calls `old_impact_fn(row)` using the import path of the pre-existing function'), so the comparative assertion is deterministically executable.
findings.finding: AC4's comparative assertion — 'the redesign beats the old scalar: median(impact_code[HIGH]) - median(impact_code[NIT]) is strictly GREATER than the old mean impact's gap on the same fixture' — does not specify where old-model scores come from. The fixture format is not stated to include pre-computed old_impact values, and no mechanism is described for invoking the old model in the test. An executor must decide whether to (a) store baseline scores in the fixture, (b) call the old impact function directly, or (c) hard-code a historical reference gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Implementation mechanics — 'a curated fixture tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `old_impact` float field to each fixture row (computed once before the redesign and committed), then the test reads it directly — no dependency on the old function being callable at test time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC[1]) that explicitly verifies the 0.33 attribute floor is absent from the code-review impact path — e.g., assert `impact_code(attrs_with_all_false_binaries) < 0.33`.
findings.finding: The 'What' section declares 'RE-ANCHOR: drop the 0.33 attribute floor' as a deliverable of this plan, but no acceptance criterion verifies that the floor has been removed from the existing code path. This is an orphaned described deliverable.
findings.location: What section ('RE-ANCHOR: drop the 0.33 attribute floor') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC[1]–AC[4])
findings.suggested_fix: In AC[1], add: 'A unit test asserts that impact_code returns 0.0 when all consequence binaries are false (no 0.33 floor applied), confirming the old attribute floor is dropped for the code-review path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the scope boundary: either remove 'co-calibrate tier values + block threshold' from 'What' (making it the child's job entirely) or update AC[4] to require that tier values are finalized/frozen here before the child tunes the threshold.
findings.finding: The 'What' section states 'co-calibrate tier values + block threshold on the labeled set' as part of this plan's deliverables, but AC[4] explicitly defers threshold tuning to a child plan ('the tuning itself is the calibration child raptorial-galloping-dragon'). An implementer cannot tell whether final tier values and the block threshold are to be finalized here or left for the child.
findings.location: What section ('RE-ANCHOR') vs. AC[4] closing clause

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback plan or expand-contract sequence for the output_schema key rename (e.g., support both keys temporarily, or document that gate + verifier must be deployed atomically with a stated rollback procedure).
findings.finding: The plan renames the output_schema key in code-review.yaml from `verification` to `code_review_verification` and changes the outputs front-matter in code-review-verify.md, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing for this schema key rename. Any environment where the verifier and gate are deployed at different times would silently lose all code-review verification output.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section — three named wiring places; no rollback/expand-contract described

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/workflow/test_code_review_workflow.py` as a consumer requiring review: confirm whether its `code_review_decide` assertions are impact-value-sensitive, and if so, update them (or explicitly annotate them as shape-only checks).
findings.finding: The existing `tests/unit/workflow/test_code_review_workflow.py` exercises `code_review_decide` as an unenumerated consumer: after the plan's change wires `impact_fn=impact_code` into `code_review_decide`, this test's `impact` assertions (which currently rely on the mean `impact` formula) will silently change their numeric values without the test being updated.
findings.location: Scope section; `tests/unit/workflow/test_code_review_workflow.py:192–193`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'Prior art considered' note to the ADR (required by AC-3) explaining why the bespoke tier values (0.3/0.6/0.9) and trigger_likelihood multipliers (1.0/0.6/0.25) were chosen over the existing `_SEV01`/`_LIKE01` ordinal maps or established frameworks (CVSS/DREAD), even if the conclusion is 'established frameworks don't separate nit from landmine because they include prod-impact axes that dilute maint findings — the same diagnosis as the Why section'.
findings.finding: The tier values (minor=0.3, moderate=0.6, serious=0.9) and trigger_likelihood multipliers (common=1.0, sometimes=0.6, rare=0.25) are committed as architectural constants with no comparison to established severity scoring frameworks (CVSS, DREAD, or the existing `_SEV01`/`_LIKE01` maps in the codebase). The plan's justification for these specific values is absent — they are stated without a 'why these numbers vs alternatives' argument.
findings.location: What section — TIER VALUES paragraph and TRIGGER-LIKELIHOOD paragraph

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
